thread.title: Hess praises present, outlines borough’s future
thread.title_full: Hess praises present, outlines borough’s future

author: mycitizensnews
title: Hess praises present, outlines borough’s future
text:
BY ANDREAS YILMA
Citizens News
NAUGATUCK — The future outlook for Naugatuck is bright and promising but is only possible with a thorough team from various areas working towards the common goal to elevate the borough.
The Naugatuck Chamber of Commerce 2024 Mayoral Breakfast took place on Nov. 13 at the Crystal Room with a packed house. The event drew more than 100 people, including community stakeholders or people involved in one of the several projects in the borough.
Mayor N. Warren “Pete” Hess said when he first became mayor about a decade ago, he had four things in his head — to fix the borough’s financial house such as growing the grand list, improving the infrastructure, not being able to accomplish all the tasks all at once and the realization that it’s not possible to accomplish the tasks alone.
“You need teams, you need partnerships, you need alliances,” Hess said. “When I look around the room here, it’s just amazing, I look at every table and I see so many people who are an integral part of our team and what we do and the entire business community and just so many people are part of the package.”
Hess acknowledged several municipal workers, developers and residents who are working in or on behalf of the borough as part of the borough team to progress multiple projects.
“I’m a project guy. I’d say 92% of my time or more is projects, not really running the town, that’s the easy part, projects, economic development, growing the grand list, helping business,” Hess said. “That’s what we do all day and then when necessary we do what it takes to run the town.”
One of the visions for the borough is to address the vacant parcel of the 36-acre Hershey property at 889 New Haven Road. The property is subject to the Transfer Act as a host of private companies over the years have been fearful of signing that piece of paper that says they’re responsible for whatever happens in the future.
“The reality is that someone’s got to jump in and solve whatever issues are there,” Hess said. “So in Naugatuck, at the borough board level we’ve decided we entered into a confidentiality agreement with Hershey.”
As the borough is evaluating the land, someone is already interested in the parcel but getting through environmental hurdles is much easier for a town. The borough also intends to retain a portion of the property for a new school, Hess said.
Another proposed project, that Hess refers to as the “icing on the cake” is the proposed pedestrian bridge over the Naugatuck River and underneath the Metro-North’s Waterbury line to connect the east to the west side of the borough. Part of that proposed development is to build a municipal parking lot by the on and off ramp exits for Route 8. This will help people not have to fight for parking downtown, Hess said.
“This will enable us to grow exponentially and to bring more people to Naugatuck and then when they come back from the train, they’re going to go into our restaurants,” Hess said.
As the borough has received funding for the design of the bridge, part of that downtown looks to increase foot traffic with a proposed greenway from Thomaston to the Nauatuck State Forest on the east side of the river in collaboration with NVCOG.
The final project Hess discussed was the new industrial park, or Industrial Park 3, where Uniroyal Chemical used to be by Elm Street Extension. International Supply, an Irish lumber company has already made part of the site its home, Hess said.
Part of the vision for the industrial park is to build a fuel cell, which needs two things, gas and proximity to an electric substation.
Eversource gas has built the borough a new gas line to its wastewater treatment plant where a fuel cell is already located. A merchant fuel cell project is part of phase one of the industrial park project where phase two will include the completion of an existing road. The borough is reserving about 20 acres on the east side of the railroad tracks for a proposed Port of Naugatuck endeavor.
“I have to look at the big picture and the future and try and move us to where we want to be,” Hess said.
